title: It Gets Quieter, and That's Okay
body:
Dear incoming Lion,

There will be a night — maybe in your second or third week — when your room feels impossibly quiet. Your roommate might be out, or maybe they're asleep, and you'll be sitting at your desk thinking, "Is this it? Is this what college is?"

I want you to know: that quiet is not a warning sign. It's just a pause. The first month is genuinely disorienting. Everyone around you seems to already have friends, plans, group chats. They don't. Most of them are pretending, just like you might be.

I didn't find my people until October. I found them in a study group for Lit Hum that turned into a weekly dinner thing. It happened slowly, and it happened because I kept showing up.

So keep showing up. To the club meetings that feel awkward. To the dining hall even when you'd rather eat alone in your room. To office hours even when you don't have a question. Columbia will meet you halfway, but you have to walk your half first.

You belong here. I promise.

— A junior who once cried in Butler Library and turned out fine

title: On Impostor Syndrome at Columbia
body:
Hey there,

Let me tell you something nobody told me: almost every single person at Columbia feels like they got in by accident. The kid in your seminar who sounds like they've read everything? They're terrified too. The person who always raises their hand? They go home and wonder if they said something stupid.

I spent my entire first semester convinced that admissions had made a mistake. I would sit in University Writing and think, "Everyone here writes better than me. Everyone here thinks faster." It was paralyzing.

Here's what I learned: impostor syndrome is not evidence that you don't belong. It's evidence that you're in a challenging place, surrounded by brilliant people, and your brain is trying to protect you by telling you to shrink.

Don't shrink.

Ask the question in class. Go to the writing center. Tell your professor you're struggling. Every single time I was honest about finding something hard, someone said, "Oh thank god, me too."

You were not admitted by accident. You are not a mistake. And the fact that you care enough to worry about whether you belong? That's exactly the kind of student Columbia needs.

Roaring for you,
A CC alum who figured it out eventually

title: Homesick and 3,000 Miles Away
body:
To the freshman reading this from a twin XL bed that doesn't feel like home yet:

I came to Columbia from a small town in Oregon. I had never been to New York. I had never shared a room. I had never been in a city where nobody knew my name.

The first two weeks, I called my mom every night. Sometimes I'd just sit on the phone and not even talk. I'd listen to her cooking dinner or talking to my dad, and it made me feel like I was still there. I was so homesick it felt physical — like something heavy pressing on my chest.

I want to be honest with you: homesickness doesn't fully go away. Not in the first semester, and maybe not in the first year. But it changes. It stops being sharp and becomes something softer. And alongside it, something new starts to grow — a sense of home here. Not a replacement for where you came from, but an addition.

New York will feel like yours eventually. Morningside Heights will feel like your neighborhood. The campus will stop feeling like a place you're visiting and start feeling like a place you live.

Give it time. Call your people when you need to. And know that missing home doesn't mean you made the wrong choice. It means you came from somewhere good — and you're building something new.

With warmth,
Someone who now calls two places home

title: You Don't Have to Have It Figured Out
body:
Dear future Columbia student,

I'm writing this from my senior year, and I want to tell you something that would have saved me a lot of anxiety: you do not need to know what you're doing.

I came in pre-med. I switched to poli sci by spring. I added a creative writing minor junior year. I'm now applying to law school. Every pivot felt like a crisis at the time, and every one turned out to be exactly right.

Columbia's Core Curriculum is actually a gift here, even when it doesn't feel like it. It forces you to read, think, and engage with things you never would have chosen. Some of my most important intellectual moments happened in classes I didn't want to take.

So if you're sitting in NSOP with a five-year plan and a color-coded spreadsheet, great. But if you're sitting there with absolutely no idea what you want to study, what you want to do, or who you want to be — also great. Maybe even better.

College is not a straight line. It's not supposed to be. Let it be messy. Let yourself change your mind. Let yourself be surprised.

The only thing you need to figure out right now is where the good coffee is. (Hint: Hungarian Pastry Shop. You're welcome.)

Go easy on yourself,
A happy senior who took the scenic route

title: Finding Your People Takes Longer Than You Think
body:
Hi,

I want to talk about something that Instagram and orientation week don't prepare you for: the timeline of real friendship at college.

During NSOP, you'll meet a hundred people. You'll exchange numbers, follow each other, make plans. Some of those connections will stick. Most won't. And that's completely normal — it's not a failure on your part.

I made my closest friends at Columbia in the weirdest ways. One was a person I kept running into at the Milstein Center printer at 2 AM. Another was someone in my FroSci section who I didn't talk to until November. My best friend is someone I met at a club I almost didn't go to because I was too tired.

The friendships that matter don't always announce themselves. They build slowly, through shared moments and repeated proximity. They come from being in the same place at the same time, again and again, until one day you realize this person knows you.

So don't panic if week three hits and you feel like everyone has a group except you. They don't — not really. And the friends you'll have at graduation will probably be people you haven't even met yet.

Be patient with the process,
A Lions fan who found her pride eventually

title: Asking for Help Is Not Weakness
body:
To whoever needs to hear this,

I almost failed my first semester at Columbia. Not because I wasn't smart enough — because I was too proud to ask for help.

I sat in lectures I didn't understand and took notes I couldn't read later. I turned in papers I knew were bad because I didn't want anyone to see a draft. I told my parents everything was great. I told my roommate I was "just tired."

By midterms, I was drowning. And the thing that saved me was not some dramatic epiphany. It was one small, terrifying moment: walking into my professor's office hours and saying, "I don't understand any of this."

She didn't judge me. She pulled out a whiteboard and spent forty minutes going through concepts with me. Then she told me about the Academic Resource Center. Then she said something I'll never forget: "The students who do best here are not the ones who already know everything. They're the ones who know how to ask."

Columbia has resources. Real ones. Tutoring, counseling, writing support, advising, peer mentoring. They exist because smart people need help. Getting help IS the smart thing.

Don't wait until you're drowning. Ask early, ask often, and ask without shame.

You've got this,
A GS student who learned the hard way

title: The City Is Your Classroom Too
body:
Dear new New Yorker,

One of the best things about Columbia is that your campus doesn't end at 120th Street. The entire city is waiting for you, and it will teach you things no lecture hall can.

In my first year, I was so focused on schoolwork that I barely left Morningside Heights. I'd go from my dorm to Hamilton to Butler and back again. New York was this incredible, overwhelming thing happening around me that I was too busy (and honestly, too scared) to engage with.

Then a friend dragged me to a free jazz show in the Village. Another time, I wandered into the Met on a rainy Sunday. I started taking the subway with no destination, just to see where I'd end up.

Those experiences shaped me as much as any class. They taught me how to be alone in a crowd. How to be curious. How to feel small in the best possible way.

You don't need money to explore New York. Museums have free nights. Parks are always free. Walking is free. The city is generous if you let it be.

So yes, study hard. Go to class. Do your problem sets. But also: take the 1 train downtown on a Saturday. Eat something you can't pronounce. Get lost on purpose.

Columbia gave me a degree. New York gave me a life.

Explore everything,
Class of '22

title: On Being the First in Your Family
body:
Hey,

If you're a first-generation college student at Columbia, this one's for you.

There are going to be moments when you feel like everyone else got a manual you didn't. They know how to talk to professors, how financial aid works, what "office hours" even means. Their parents went to college. Their siblings went to college. They have a vocabulary for this experience that you're still learning.

I know because I lived it. My parents didn't finish high school. When I got into Columbia, they were proud but also confused. They couldn't help me pick classes or understand why I was stressed about a Core requirement. The distance between my world and theirs grew in ways I didn't expect, and that was one of the hardest parts.

But here's what I also learned: being first-gen is a superpower. You notice things other people take for granted. You're resourceful in ways that come from having to figure things out alone. You bring a perspective to classroom discussions that is genuinely valuable, even when it doesn't feel that way.

Find your first-gen community here. It exists, and it's strong. And remember: every time you feel lost, you're navigating something genuinely new. That's not weakness. That's courage.

Proudly first-gen,
SEAS '25

title: It's Okay to Not Be Okay
body:
Dear incoming student,

I'm going to skip the part where I tell you how great Columbia is. You already know that. Instead, I want to talk about the parts that are hard, because someone should.

There will be weeks when everything piles up — papers, midterms, club commitments, social obligations — and you'll feel like you're running on empty. There will be days when you wake up and the thought of going to class feels impossible. There will be moments when you look around at everyone else functioning and wonder what's wrong with you.

Nothing is wrong with you. College is a massive life transition, and sometimes the weight of it is more than you can carry alone.

I went to Counseling and Psychological Services during my sophomore year. I put it off for months because I thought my problems weren't "serious enough." When I finally went, the therapist said something simple that changed everything: "You don't have to be in crisis to deserve support."

Whatever you're feeling — overwhelmed, anxious, lonely, lost, numb — it's valid. And there are people at Columbia whose entire job is to help you with exactly that.

You're allowed to struggle. You're allowed to ask for help. And doing so doesn't make you weak. It makes you wise.

Taking care of yourself is part of the work,
A fellow Lion who's still figuring it out

title: The Dining Hall Is a Social Space (Use It)
body:
Quick tip from someone who learned this way too late:

Go to the dining hall even when you're not hungry. Seriously.

JJ's, Ferris Booth, John Jay — these aren't just places to eat. They're where casual friendships become real ones. Every meaningful relationship I built at Columbia was strengthened over a meal where neither of us had anywhere to be.

In the first few weeks, it's tempting to grab food and take it back to your room. I get it. The dining hall can feel overwhelming when you don't know anyone. But here's the trick: neither does anyone else. If you sit down next to someone from your floor and say "hey, mind if I join you?" — I have never, not once, seen someone say no.

Some of my best conversations at Columbia happened at 10 PM over bad coffee and mediocre pasta. Those unplanned, lingering dinners are where you find out who someone really is, past the surface-level orientation small talk.

So eat with people. Eat with different people. Stay longer than you think you should.

Bon appétit,
Someone who misses John Jay dining (never thought I'd say that)

title: Your Identity Has a Place Here
body:
To the incoming student who's wondering if there's room for all of who you are:

There is. I promise.

I came to Columbia as a queer student from a conservative town. I had spent my whole life carefully managing what people knew about me. College was supposed to be different, but I was terrified it wouldn't be.

It was different. Not immediately — not in some dramatic, movie-montage way. But gradually, as I found spaces where I could be fully myself. Cultural organizations, affinity groups, late-night conversations with people who understood without me having to explain.

Columbia isn't perfect. No institution is. You might encounter ignorance, microaggressions, moments that sting. But you'll also find communities that will hold you up. People who share your experience. Mentors who've walked your path.

Whatever part of your identity feels heavy right now — your race, your sexuality, your religion, your background, your neurodivergence, anything — there are people here who will see you. Not despite who you are, but because of it.

Bring your whole self. This campus needs exactly that.

With love and solidarity,
A Columbian who found their people

title: The Core Will Surprise You
body:
Dear future Columbian,

You're about to start the Core Curriculum, and if you're anything like me, you have opinions about it already. Maybe you're excited. Maybe you're dreading Lit Hum. Maybe you're wondering why an engineering student needs to read Homer.

I was firmly in the "this is pointless" camp. I wanted to study computer science, not debate whether Achilles was justified. I walked into my first Literature Humanities class with my arms crossed (metaphorically, mostly).

And then something happened. Slowly, without my permission, those books started to matter. Not all of them — I still don't love Virgil, sorry — but enough of them. I found myself thinking about the Iliad while walking across campus. I brought up Aristotle in a conversation at dinner and meant it.

The Core doesn't just teach you content. It gives you a shared language with every other Columbia student. You'll make references in conversation that land perfectly because the person across from you read the same thing. It's this invisible thread that connects you to people across years and disciplines.

Give it a real chance. Even the parts that bore you. Especially the parts that bore you. You might be surprised by what stays with you.

With reluctant gratitude for Lit Hum,
A CS major who accidentally fell in love with philosophy
